description: This figure presents finite element simulation results showing the evolution of dislocation density distributions and phase-specific strengthening contributions in TiB₂-reinforced steels during tensile deformation. Panels (a) and (b) display simulated dislocation density increment (ρ-ρ₀) maps at global strains of ε=0.0%, 5.0%, and 11.0% for conventional Fe–TiB₂ steel and the present low-density steel (LDS), respectively, with the color scale ranging from 2 to 23×10¹⁴ m⁻². The simulations reveal that the conventional steel develops narrow shear bands oriented at approximately 45° with isolated undeformed zones (highlighted by red circles) indicating stress shielding, whereas the heterogeneous LDS matrix exhibits more uniform dislocation distributions due to microscopic strain partitioning between soft austenite and hard δ-ferrite. Panel (c) shows the calculated true stress-strain curve compared with experimental data, decomposed into volume-fraction-weighted strengthening contributions from austenite (blue), δ-ferrite (red), and elastic load-bearing TiB₂ particles (orange), demonstrating that δ-ferrite provides a constant baseline contribution of approximately 170 MPa while austenite delivers increasing work hardening.
